title: Heaps / Priority Queues
description: Master C# PriorityQueue (.NET 6+), SortedSet as heap alternative, and manual heap operations for coding interviews.

sections.title: PriorityQueue<TElement, TPriority> (.NET 6+)
sections.explanation: C# .NET 6+ provides `PriorityQueue<TElement, TPriority>` which is a min-heap by default. `Enqueue(element, priority)` adds with a priority. `Dequeue()` removes and returns the element with the lowest priority. `Peek()` views without removing. For a max-heap, negate the priority. This is the cleanest heap in C# for interviews.

exercise.prompt: Create a PriorityQueue<string, int> for a task scheduler. Enqueue: ("email", 3), ("bug fix", 1), ("meeting", 2), ("deploy", 1). Dequeue all items and print each on its own line (lowest priority number = highest urgency, processed first).

validation.message: Tasks in priority order: bug fix and deploy (pri 1), meeting (pri 2), email (pri 3).
Use PriorityQueue with Enqueue and Dequeue.

exercise.hint: PriorityQueue is a min-heap. Priority 1 items come out before priority 2 and 3.

sections.title: Max-Heap via Negated Priority
sections.explanation: PriorityQueue is min-heap only. For max-heap behavior, negate the priority: `pq.Enqueue(val, -val)`. This makes larger values come out first. For non-numeric priorities, implement `IComparer<T>` and pass to the constructor. This pattern is essential for top-K largest, merge-K sorted, and median-finding problems.

exercise.prompt: Given int[] scores = {72, 95, 88, 63, 91, 85}, use a PriorityQueue as a max-heap (negate priorities) to extract and print the top 3 scores in descending order, one per line.

validation.message: Use PriorityQueue with negated priorities for max-heap behavior.

exercise.hint: Enqueue each score with -score as priority. Dequeue 3 times to get the 3 largest.

sections.title: SortedSet as Heap Alternative
sections.explanation: `SortedSet<T>` maintains elements in sorted order with O(log n) add/remove. Use `.Min` and `.Max` for smallest/largest, mimicking heap Peek. However, SortedSet requires unique elements—for duplicates, use a wrapper tuple like `(value, index)`. It supports `GetViewBetween()` for range queries. Use when you need both min and max access.

exercise.prompt: Use a SortedSet<(int val, int idx)> to simulate a min-heap that handles duplicates. Insert values {4, 1, 7, 1, 3} with unique indices. Extract the minimum 3 times (remove Min), printing each value.

validation.message: Use SortedSet with tuple for duplicate support, Min for peek, Remove for extract.

exercise.hint: Add (nums[i], i) for each element. heap.Min.val gives the minimum. Remove(heap.Min) extracts it.

sections.title: Top-K Pattern with PriorityQueue
sections.explanation: The top-K pattern: maintain a min-heap of size K. For each new element, if heap size < K, add it. If element > heap minimum, remove min and add element. At the end, the heap contains the K largest elements. Time: O(n log k). This is more efficient than sorting when K << n.

exercise.prompt: Implement `int KthLargest(int[] nums, int k)` that returns the k-th largest element using a min-heap of size k. After processing all elements, the heap top is the answer. Test with nums = {3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4} for k=2 (answer: 5) and k=4 (answer: 3).

validation.message: Use PriorityQueue, evict when size exceeds k, return the top at the end.

exercise.hint: Enqueue each with itself as priority. If Count > k, Dequeue (removes smallest). Final Dequeue is kth largest.
